text:
Network services are growing rapidly and advanced types of cyberattacks are emerging. Therefore, building strong intrusion detection systems (IDS) is crucial. In anomaly detection using machine learning, high dimensional datasets are usually feature rich, resulting in feature sets with many irrelevant, redundant or noise features, which results in a decrease in the detection accuracy and overfitting. In this paper, a new hybrid feature selection method called IGRF-RFE is proposed for improving the performance of multi-class network intrusion detection classification using multilayer perceptron (MLP) classifier.

Abstract This paper proposes a new approach, named IGRF-RFE, that combines the filter methods Information Gain and Random Forest for feature reduction, along with a wrapper based Recursive Feature Elimination, in order to design an efficient MLP classifier. In this context, the IGRF-RFE approach works in two phases: in the first phase the most significant features are obtained by applying two filter methods to reduce any kind of bias in the feature selection, namely Information Gain (IG) and Random Forest (RF), so that the most relevant features are chosen while avoiding bias through the use of relevant indicators. In the second phase, a wrapper based Recursive Feature Elimination (RFE) is used on the obtained set in order to remove the less significant features to the chosen architecture of the MLP classifier. The IGRF-RFE approach also includes the complete preprocessing stage, such as the elimination of redundant samples to avoid the ranking bias, and the resampling techniques to solve the problem of imbalance between classes.

Performance was evaluated using the modern UNSW-NB15 dataset. It was shown that IGRF-RFE can significantly reduce the feature set dimensionality from 42 to 23 features. Furthermore, the subsequent MLP classifier showed an increase in its multi-class accuracy from 82.25% to 84.24%. Therefore, the hybrid filter – wrapper approach of IGRF-RFE achieved a better balance between the speed of feature selection process and the accuracy of the feature subset identified in contrast to the corresponding filter and wrapper approaches used alone.
explanation: The passage is highly coherent, uses credible IDS terminology, and cites a real dataset (UNSW-NB15) with plausible feature counts and accuracy improvements. While this could be from a human author, the dense, formulaic structure and lack of highly novel methodological details are also typical of AI-generated text.
